Framingham study

Data description

Hypotheses

statistical hypotheses

difference over time periods

Methods

table 1.

Detail how you got to your final dataset, along with counts at each step.

Data comes from a subset of the Framingham Heart Study, with 4,434 individuals followed for 3 time periods, approximately 6 years apart, ranging from 1956 to 1968. For the risk factor analysis, we excluded individuals who had already had a stroke before the beginnng of the study (TIMESTRK=0), only considered period 1, in order to capture baseline characteristics, and censored all individuals at 10 years (3652 days), resulting in 4,402 remaining individuals. We disregarded other events measured in the data. We also considered complete case analysis, removing individuals with missing information on the covariates we are interested in, and stratified the data into a male group and female group, with final sample sizes of 1,897 and 2,378 respectively. To analyze change in risk factors, we used data on the same individuals for periods 1 and 3 for consistent interpretation.

table1_m <- framdat_m |>
  tbl_summary(
    include = c(TOTCHOL, AGE, SYSBP, DIABP, CURSMOKE, CIGPDAY, BMI, DIABETES, BPMEDS, HEARTRTE,
                GLUCOSE, educ, PREVCHD, PREVAP, PREVMI,  PREVHYP, STROKE, HYPERTEN, time_obs 
    ), # Specify variables to include (optional)
    missing = "ifany"                      # Display missing values if any exist
  )

table1_f <- framdat_f |>
  tbl_summary(
    include = c(TOTCHOL, AGE, SYSBP, DIABP, CURSMOKE, CIGPDAY, BMI, DIABETES, BPMEDS, HEARTRTE,
                GLUCOSE, educ, PREVCHD, PREVAP, PREVMI,  PREVHYP, STROKE, HYPERTEN, time_obs 
    ), # Specify variables to include (optional)
    missing = "ifany"                      # Display missing values if any exist
  )

tbl_merge(
  tbls = list(table1_m, table1_f),
  tab_spanner = c("**Male**", "**Female**")
)
## The number rows in the tables to be merged do not match, which may result in
## rows appearing out of order.

##   `quiet=TRUE` to silence message.

1 Median (Q1, Q3); n (%)

To examine which risk profiles increase the probability of stroke in 10 years, we first visualized the survival curves for different levels of the risk factors, showing that there are visible differences in the survival curves and that the risk factors are worth investigating. We are primarily interested in studying age, high systolic blood pressure (>180 mmHg) and diabetes as risk factors, but also want to identify whether blood pressure medication use, BMI, cholesterol levels, smoking status (current vs. not) and coronary heart disease also significantly contribute to the probability of stroke. To identify significant risk factors and compute their effects on the probability of stroke, we created Cox PH models for the female group and the male group, selecting risk factors to report on using backward stepwise selection with the Akaike Information Criterion, using systolic blood pressure, age, and diabetes as the null model, since we are primarily interested in reporting on these. For males, the model with the lowest AIC included age, systolic blood pressure, diabetes, and smoking status, meaning that this model fits the data better and includes more predictive variables than other models. For females, the model with the lowest AIC was the null model. To compare groups, we assessed Cox PH survival curves and risk profile probabilities using the male model’s variables including smoking.

For both sex groups, and for ages 40,50, and 60, we were primarily interested in the stroke risk for the following profiles: someone with diabetes, someone with high blood pressure (we used 160 to compute our risk profiles), and someone with both. Additionally, because smoking was found to be predictive, we also considered the risk profile of a smoker, as well as a smoker with diabetes and high blood pressure.

library(survival)
library(survminer)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(gridExtra)
## 
## Attaching package: 'gridExtra'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     combine
# --- Helper: create quantile groups for continuous variables ---
quartile_group <- function(x) {
  cut(x,
      breaks = quantile(x, probs = 0:4/4, na.rm = TRUE),
      labels = c("Q1","Q2","Q3","Q4"),
      include.lowest = TRUE)
}

# --- Prepare grouping variables ---
framdat2 <- framdat %>%
  mutate(
    AGE_Q      = quartile_group(AGE),
    SYSBP_Q    = quartile_group(SYSBP),
    BMI_Q      = quartile_group(BMI),
    TOTCHOL_Q  = quartile_group(TOTCHOL),
    DIABETES   = factor(DIABETES,   labels = c("No","Yes")),
    BPMEDS     = factor(BPMEDS,     labels = c("No","Yes")),
    CURSMOKE   = factor(CURSMOKE,   labels = c("No","Yes")),
    PREVCHD    = factor(PREVCHD,    labels = c("No","Yes"))
  )

# --- Fit KM models ---
# Adjust 'TIME' and 'EVENT' to your actual survival time and status columns
fits <- list(
  AGE     = survfit(Surv(time_obs, stroke_10yr) ~ AGE_Q,     data = framdat2),
  SYSBP   = survfit(Surv(time_obs, stroke_10yr) ~ SYSBP_Q,   data = framdat2),
  BMI     = survfit(Surv(time_obs, stroke_10yr) ~ BMI_Q,     data = framdat2),
  TOTCHOL = survfit(Surv(time_obs, stroke_10yr) ~ TOTCHOL_Q, data = framdat2),
  DIABETES = survfit(Surv(time_obs,stroke_10yr) ~ DIABETES,  data = framdat2),
  BPMEDS  = survfit(Surv(time_obs, stroke_10yr) ~ BPMEDS,    data = framdat2),
  CURSMOKE = survfit(Surv(time_obs, stroke_10yr) ~ CURSMOKE,  data = framdat2),
  PREVCHD = survfit(Surv(time_obs, stroke_10yr) ~ PREVCHD,   data = framdat2)
)

# --- Plot settings ---
palette_q4  <- c("#2166AC","#4DAC26","#D6604D","#762A83")  # 4-level continuous
palette_bin <- c("#2166AC","#D6604D")                       # binary

common_opts <- list(
  conf.int    = FALSE,
  risk.table  = FALSE,
  ggtheme     = theme_classic(base_size = 8),
  legend.labs = NULL,   # overridden per plot
  font.main   = c(9, "bold"),
  font.x      = 8,
  font.y      = 8,
  font.legend = 7,
  legend      = "bottom",
  xlab        = "Time",
  ylab        = "Survival"
)

make_plot <- function(fit, title, labs, pal) {
  ggsurvplot(
    fit,
    data        = framdat2,
    conf.int    = FALSE,
    censor=FALSE,
    risk.table  = FALSE,
    palette     = pal,
    legend.labs = labs,
    legend      = "bottom",
    title       = title,
    xlab        = "Time",
    ylab        = "Survival",
    ggtheme     = theme_classic(base_size = 8) +
      theme(
        plot.title      = element_text(size = 9, face = "bold", hjust = 0.5),
        legend.key.size = unit(0.35, "cm"),
        legend.text     = element_text(size = 6.5),
        legend.title    = element_blank(),
        axis.title      = element_text(size = 7.5)
      )
  )$plot
}

plots <- list(
  make_plot(fits$AGE,      "Age",                   c("Q1","Q2","Q3","Q4"), palette_q4),
  make_plot(fits$SYSBP,    "Systolic BP",           c("Q1","Q2","Q3","Q4"), palette_q4),
  make_plot(fits$BMI,      "BMI",                   c("Q1","Q2","Q3","Q4"), palette_q4),
  make_plot(fits$TOTCHOL,  "Total Cholesterol",     c("Q1","Q2","Q3","Q4"), palette_q4),
  make_plot(fits$DIABETES, "Diabetes",              c("No","Yes"),          palette_bin),
  make_plot(fits$BPMEDS,   "BP Medications",        c("No","Yes"),          palette_bin),
  make_plot(fits$CURSMOKE, "Current Smoker",        c("No","Yes"),          palette_bin),
  make_plot(fits$PREVCHD,  "Prev. CHD",             c("No","Yes"),          palette_bin)
)

# --- Combine into 2x4 grid ---
km_grid <- gridExtra::grid.arrange(
  grobs  = plots,
  ncol   = 4,
  nrow   = 2
)
